summary: Gruber clarifies a widely misunderstood aspect of subscription music services like Napster To Go: when you cancel your subscription, all downloaded music stops working. He argues that $180/year is far more than most people spend on music downloads, noting that most iPod owners spend under $100/year on iTunes. He points out that subscription music still requires per-track fees for burning CDs, and that Napster's marketing deliberately obscures these limitations in fine print. He concludes that people who invest in subscriptions will spend thousands over a decade and own nothing, while CD and iTunes buyers retain their purchases permanently.
key_points: Subscription music services are rental, not ownership — all music stops working when you cancel
Napster To Go's marketing buries the critical limitation that continued access requires maintaining a subscription
Most iPod owners spend well under $100/year on iTunes, making a $180/year subscription a poor value comparison
Most adults already own the bulk of the music they'll ever want from prior CD purchases
A decade of subscription payments yields thousands spent with nothing owned, while purchased music persists indefinitely
money_quotes: If you drop your subscription, all of the music you've downloaded ceases to function.
In other words, with a subscription service, you're renting music, not buying it.
I buy singles online, but when I buy albums, I buy CDs. I.e. I use iTMS in addition to buying CDs, not instead of.
I think $180 per year is just way more than most people are willing to spend for rented music downloads, regardless how many songs they can download.
Most of us over the age of, say, 25 or so, already own most of the music we're ever going to want.
A kid today who puts his money into a subscription service like Napster To Go is going to wind up spending a couple of thousand dollars over the next decade, and yet own nothing.
key_insight: Subscription music services obscure through marketing what is fundamentally a rental model, and for most consumers the economics of ownership — whether through CDs or iTunes — are far more favorable than perpetual subscription fees for music you never actually own.
